questions.question: Why is modulo hashing painful when a fleet grows or shrinks?
options: A small node-count change can remap a large fraction of keys, causing heavy movement or cold misses.
It prevents hashing keys at all.
It only works for three servers.
It requires a control plane on every request.
questions.explanation: Modulo placement is simple, but topology changes can invalidate placement broadly.

questions.question: What is the main promise of consistent hashing?
options: Topology changes should only remap a bounded portion of keys instead of almost all of them.
Every node always gets exactly the same load.
Hot keys disappear automatically.
Health checks are no longer necessary.
questions.explanation: The win is reduced remapping churn during node changes.

questions.question: Why are virtual nodes often added to a consistent-hash ring?
options: They smooth distribution so physical nodes own a more even share of the ring.
They remove the need for hashing.
They guarantee no node can ever fail.
They make placement impossible to explain.
questions.explanation: More virtual points usually reduce imbalance from coarse placement.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with increasing virtual nodes aggressively?
options: Balance improves, but metadata and control-plane complexity grow.
Remap percentage always becomes 100 percent.
Requests can no longer be routed locally.
Hot keys become harmless.
questions.explanation: Virtual nodes are helpful, but they are not free to manage.

questions.question: Which metric best validates a planned rebalance before rollout?
options: Expected remap percentage and ownership histogram by node.
Only the number of markdown files in the repo.
Average laptop battery level.
TLS certificate age.
questions.explanation: You want to know how much movement and imbalance the change will create.

questions.question: Why does consistent hashing not solve pathological hot keys by itself?
options: One extremely hot key can still overload whichever node owns it even if average placement is balanced.
The ring can only place cold keys.
Hot keys cannot be hashed.
Consistent hashing requires every key to be unique globally.
questions.explanation: Distribution of average load is different from handling concentrated hotspots.

questions.question: What is the most useful mitigation for a cold-cache storm after a node add or remove?
options: Stage the rollout and prewarm or migrate selectively instead of switching all traffic at once.
Disable health checks.
Reduce virtual nodes to one.
Route all traffic to the newest node.
questions.explanation: Controlled rollout reduces miss spikes and recovery pain.

questions.question: What is the strongest first clarification when asked about consistent hashing?
options: Ask whether the placement is for caches, durable data, or request ownership, because rebalance cost differs a lot.
Assume every workload needs the same ring design.
Avoid talking about node churn.
Start by drawing a ring and stop there.
questions.explanation: The cost of movement and the safety requirements depend on what the placement controls.
